data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T01:46:52.008Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were always implemented as sync-over-async internally. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely.


The Cosmos DB provider's underlying SDK is async-only, so methods like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` would internally block on async calls. This sync-over-async pattern was deprecated and flagged as problematic, and the breaking change removes it entirely for the Cosmos provider.

Let me trace through the actual timeline more carefully. In EF Core 9, the Cosmos provider started throwing `InvalidOperationException` for synchronous operations by default because of the dangers of sync-over-async like deadlocks and thread pool starvation, though there was an opt-in to temporarily re-enable it. I need to verify what happens in the .NET 11 release cycle.

So the breaking change is that all synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, and `Count()`, along with `SaveChanges()`, will throw `InvalidOperationException` at runtime when the sync-over-async opt-in is completely removed. The fix is straightforward—convert everything to async equivalents: `ToListAsync()`, `FirstAsync()`, `CountAsync()`, and `SaveChangesAsync()`.
